data.title: LivaNova PLC: Excellent Revenue Earner, Some Short-Term Litigation Concerns
data.description:
LivaNova PLC (LIVN) is a London headquartered company that develops and markets Cardiopulmonary and Neuromodulation devices. It also used to have an Advanced Circulatory Support business that was wound down in January 2024 to help them focus on core competencies. The company was formed in 2015 through the merger of Cyberonics (U.S.) and Sorin Group (Italy). The following description of the company’s products is adapted from their latest 10-K.

The Cardiopulmonary segment

The core idea of this segment is to produce devices that assist a surgeon in carrying out a cardiopulmonary bypass (CPB) procedure. The core product in this segment is the Essenz Perfusion System. This is LivaNova’s next-generation Heart Lung Machine, or HLM. An HLM is basically a combination of two machines, both of which together replace the functions of the heart and the lung of a patient during a CPB procedure such as coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG) and valve repair/replacement.

These HLMs include heater-coolers that regulate body temperature, which is critical during complex surgeries.

The CPB procedure also uses oxygenators which exchange gases (oxygen and CO₂) when the patient’s lungs are unable to do so. LivaNova’s Inspire product line oxygenators are disposable devices used during extracorporeal circulation (ECMO) or in the HLM.

The company also offers perfusion tubing systems and cannulae that help in connecting and circulating blood through the bypass circuit, ensuring proper flow and delivery to the surgical field. They have various configurations for different patient populations and surgical needs.

The company also has autotransfusion systems that collect the patient’s own blood lost during surgery and reinfuse it back into the patient. This helps conserve blood loss and reduces the need for donor transfusions.

LivaNova is a leader in cardiac surgery support technologies, offering a full suite of tools to improve surgical outcomes and patient safety in cardiopulmonary care.

The Neuromodulation segment

LivaNova’s Neuromodulation segment develops devices that are implanted into a patient and can deliver electrical stimulation for various CNS diseases. This stimulation is delivered to targeted nerves for the treatment of drug-resistant epilepsy (DRE), depression with treatment-resistant features (DTD), and obstructive sleep apnea (OSA).

Vagus Nerve Stimulation (VNS) Therapy

At the heart of the segment is the VNS Therapy System, comprising an implantable pulse generator, a lead connected to the vagus nerve, and a set of surgical tools and software.

VNS Therapy is FDA-approved for DRE in patients as young as four, and is also approved in the EU for treatment-resistant depression (TRD). The company sells devices like SenTiva and AspireSR, which offer advanced features such as AutoStim. This can detect how a heart rate changes during seizures, and can deliver just the right sort/amount of stimulation in response to that. These systems are also MRI-compatible and support flexible therapy scheduling.

LivaNova's SenTiva line is the smallest and lightest among implantables in this class and supports advanced features like day/night programming.

Epilepsy (DRE)

VNS Therapy is widely used after failure of two antiseizure medications (ASMs). In 1997, the company got the first approval for a medical device targeting DRE in the USA. The system is very effective in syndromes like Dravet and Lennox-Gastaut, and has received good CMS reimbursement in recent years. The company’s models include Demipulse (Model 103), AspireSR (Model 106), SenTiva (Model 1000), and the SenTiva Duo (Model 1000D).

Depression (DTD)

The VNS therapy was approved by the FDA in 2005. However, CMS refused to cover it. This has caused limited adoption of VNS in DTD.

IN 2017, following promising results from a large naturalistic study, CMS launched the Coverage with Evidence Development (CED) pathway, leading to the RECOVER trial.

The RECOVER study (up to 1,000 patients with unipolar or bipolar depression) failed to meet the primary endpoint, although it met a number of key secondary endpoints successfully. Further studies showed clinically meaningful differences between active and sham VNS therapies. Based on all this, while the company is still pursuing CMS coverage, LivaNova plans to reduce investment in the DTD program in 2025.

Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)

LivaNova is also advancing the aura6000 System, which stimulates the hypoglossal nerve to activate tongue and palate muscles during sleep. This system came to LivanOva through the 2018 acquisition of ImThera. The device targets moderate-to-severe OSA in patients who are CPAP-intolerant. The OSPREY study met the primary efficacy and safety endpoints in this indication, and the company plans to submit a PMA in 2025.

Other events

The company began launching parts of the Essenz Perfusion System in stages over a few years. In 2022, it launched the Essenz Patient Monitor system, which is a monitoring system that displays data to patients in an easily comprehensible format. The monitor was already being used in some US and European facilities.

In 2022, as well, the company went through a critical period when the FDA had it recall several hundred blood bypass pump systems the company had sold since 2019. The FDA classified this as a Class I recall, the most serious type of FDA recall. This LifeSPARC system is used during the CPB procedures to pump blood through a circuit outside the body. A possible software malfunction in this circuit resulted in 66 complaints, but fortunately for the company, no deaths.

In 2023, the company got approval for its Essenz In-Line Blood Monitor (ILBM), part of the entire Essenz system. The monitor allows for continuous monitoring of blood, an essential tool for CPB procedures, unlike the standard blood gas analyzers that only provide data from blood drawn in a sample.

Competition

NeuroPace offers the RNS System, an FDA approved closed-loop intracranial neurostimulation device to treat adults with partial-onset seizures. This device is implanted into the brain and is, therefore, a much more invasive procedure than VNS. However, RNS has certain advantages too - it can monitor brain activity and record EEG data.

MedTronic offers the Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS) device, which has implanted thalamic electrodes and is approved for epilepsy in the EU, and under humanitarian exemption in the US. The device was very recently approved in the US for Parkinson’s patients.

LivaNova is a global leader in VNS, which remains a less invasive, widely reimbursed, and well-studied option compared to intracranial competitors.

In CPB, LivaNova’s Essenz competes most directly with Getinge’s HL 40 in modularity and flexibility, Terumo APS 1 in monitoring and safety, and Spectrum in digital integration. The HL 40 is widely adopted globally, especially in Europe. The Advanced Perfusion System 1 (APS 1) has a strong presence in the US and Japan.

Revenues

The company reported that fourth-quarter revenue rose 3.8% year-over-year to $321.8 million. This was driven by a 9.9% jump in Cardiopulmonary sales, while Neuromodulation revenue was flat. While there was growth in the US market, there were declines in Europe and other markets.

GAAP EPS for the quarter came in at $1.02 versus $0.30 a year ago, while adjusted EPS was $0.81, down slightly from $0.87. GAAP operating income improved significantly to $37.0 million from a loss of $88.0 million in Q4 2023. There was a significant rise in GAAP EPS, which would imply either operational improvements or one-time gains of some sort. However, the decline in adjusted EPS, while small, probably results from flat neuromodulation growth coupled with what has happened at many other global healthcare companies I covered recently - currency headwinds.

For the full year, LivaNova posted $1.25 billion in revenue, up 8.7% on a reported basis and 10.7% organically. Here, too, the Cardiopulmonary segment led growth with a 13.1% increase, while Neuromodulation rose 6.6%. GAAP EPS for the year was $1.16 (up from $0.32 in 2023), and adjusted EPS rose to $3.38 from $2.80. The company generated $183 million in operating cash flow and $162.9 million in adjusted free cash flow.

The company expects 2025 revenue growth of 6–7% organically and adjusted EPS in the range of $3.65 to $3.75. Free cash flow is projected between $135 million and $155 million.

Financials

LIVN has a market cap of $1.84bn and a cash balance of $428.9mn. SG&A and R&D expenses were $143.8mn and $41.3mn in the December quarter. In the same quarter, their CFO was $78.7mn, and capex was ($10.4mn), giving them a positive FCF of $68.3mn, and thus, an indefinite cash runway.

Risks

In their earnings call, among other issues, analysts asked about the SNIA liability ruling.

Very recently, the Italian Supreme Court ruled that LIVN is liable to pay $363mn in connection with “environmental liabilities tied to SNIA, its former parent company.” While the company is appealing, in my opinion, the ruling will go against them, and they will have to pay a substantial chunk of their bank balance to settle or pay the damages. This is a core risk.

The other risk, or rather, frustration, is the DTD segment, which started off well but then failed to sustain itself after CMS coverage denial and poor trial data. That is reflective of the generally lackluster performance of the neuromodulation segment.

Bottom-line

LIVN looks like a solid earner with a huge revenue stream vis-a-vis its market cap. The stock nearly halved after the Italian ruling, but I guess it will find bottom by the end of H1 2025, once the appeals are heard and decided. We may take another look at LIVN then.

data.title: Rates Spark: Demand For Safety To Stay
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Michiel Tukker, Benjamin Schroeder

US Treasuries give the pause trade a mild thumbs up

Risk is back on since the tariff pause news, with material rallies on equity markets (the ones open) and some retightening in credit spreads (high yield 5yr CTX is tighter by a notable 60bp). This is understandable, as the general sense of anxiety has diminished. Market rates though are less excited, which is a reminder that there remains a material elevation of angst. This also makes sense, as the tariff barriers remain, albeit reduced in certain areas and only for 90 days.

Front-end market rates were initially up by 20bp but have since been pared back by 5bp. So still up, but not by enough to mark a material change in overall perception. There has been even less reaction on the 10yr yield, which now just feels comfortable up in the 4.4% area. Bonds are signalling that the pause is significant, yet not much has fundamentally changed. This could shift if the 90-day pause becomes permanent. Until then, the US bond market remains somewhat negatively impacted by the initial tariff actions.

Good news for the EU, but risk sentiment remains wary

A pause for now is good news for the eurozone and the 10Y swap rate is back at levels from before "Liberation Day". The significant rise in UST yields played an important role in this. The front end of the curve still trades at lower rates, reflecting the still-outstanding risks on investors’ minds. Markets are reverting to an ECB landing zone between 1.75-2%, a range which was held for many months previously. But we remain wary that the situation could easily worsen again. The trade wars are still far from over and the EU still faces higher tariffs, especially on the car industry. Meanwhile, China and the US are clashing, and an end to their conflict does not seem to be in sight.

The increased volatility will also remain, preventing a full restoration of risk sentiment. Markets will not easily forget these episodes with wide market swings, and thus, the demand for safe assets should remain elevated. 10Y Bunds, for one, significantly outperformed swaps during the peak stress episode underscoring their safe haven role, even if they are now trading above the swap curve again.

Thursday's events and market view

With markets relieved by Trump’s turnaround on tariffs, the main data points to digest come from the US in the form of the CPI and jobless claims numbers. The headline CPI is expected to fall to 2.5%, but with still substantial tariffs incoming, it will have little signalling effect. More notable is that the monthly core CPI is seen at a relatively high 0.3% month-on-month. The afternoon will see a number of Fed speakers, including Logan Schmid and Goolsbee.

In primary markets, Spain will auction 4y, 8y and 10y bonds for up to €6.5bn in total. The US Treasury will auction US$22bn in 30y bonds which will be closely scrutinised. Last night’s 10y auction did see strong demand with also a high share of non-dealer demand.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Q1 2025 Credit Commentary Trump 2.0 Effect On Munis And Other Challenges
data.description:
By Patricia Healy, CFA

The events of the quarter include DOGE and tariff actions, the reassessing of and halting of subsidies, and the functioning and funding of major spending programs, e.g. Medicaid and education priorities, among many other changes from Trump 2.0 that will challenge municipalities. These challenges come on top of the runoff of pandemic aid, increasing natural disasters and weather risks, and the need for continued vigilance to protect against cyber threats. Change is constant, but so many changes all at once are unusual. Thankfully, many municipalities have amassed strong reserves and practice good budget management.

Trump 2.0 has many complaining and hopefully thinking about better ways to do things instead of following the status quo. Some have called the moves revolutionary – which may be a good thing, since we have had a slow creep of policies over the past 30+ years that are in need of review. The recent actions by DOGE are seemingly more about reducing headcounts and rooting out fraud and less about reimagining how government can run more efficiently. That could be the focus of the next stage, and Congress may reevaluate legislation regarding administrative agencies.

As we have noted in the past, municipal management is enduring the burden of increased bureaucracy without having to address conflicting or old requirements. Many municipalities are buried in paperwork rather than our building capital projects and providing useful services. Philip Howard and Common Good are working to educate federal, state and local governments and other leaders on ways to approach the problem. See “Federal Government Efficiency Commission Trickle-Down to State and Local Municipalities.”

We did not foresee the extent of Trump’s ever-changing tariffs– and the long-term ramifications, let alone the effects of the short-term turmoil, remain to be seen. The release on “Liberation Day” contained items not expected, and companies, municipalities, analysts, and pundits continue to discuss how the changes could affect operations and profitability. See Cumberland Chief Economist David Berson’s informative commentary “Tariffs!”

Municipalities were already grappling with budget challenges with the end of pandemic aid, along with inflation in goods, services, and especially labor. Many had planned for the inevitability of these developments, and many have large reserves. Some have needed to make cuts, and others have had to or plan to draw down reserves and raise taxes. In some cases, there will be downgrades unless the affected municipality can show a clear path to restoring reserves and budget balance.

Kansas was the first state to have an S&P negative credit action, driven in part by potential Trump administration policies that could dampen economic growth and pressure finances. In April, the state’s AA- S&P rating outlook was reduced to stable from positive, reflecting an evolving budget environment created by the state’s recently enacted tax relief package and potential actions taken at the federal level that could create a weaker revenue environment and budget pressure for the state, making it more challenging for it to address projected structural budget imbalances.

Meanwhile, Washington DC's rating was put on review for a downgrade by Moody’s, given the drastic cuts to the federal workforce, which have an outsized impact on the District’s economy, finances and employment. Fitch also placed DC on rating watch negative, noting yet another challenge to the district of the potential reduction in federal funding that the federal government may impose.

The wildfires in Los Angeles resulted in an S&P two-notch downgrade to the Los Angeles Department of Water and Power’s power system bonds to A from AA- and its water system bonds to AA- from AA+, as the systems share management and other resources. Both were also put on CreditWatch negative. In addition to the potential liability for possibly being responsible for the fires, the rating agency noted the increasing frequency and severity of highly destructive wildfires within LADWP's service territory and their recent spread into more urban areas. Final determination of liability may result in the removal of the CreditWatch negative. Other rating agencies also took negative actions on the credits.

Trickle-down

Changes to federal funding of state and local governments are being discussed, including large programs related to Medicaid and education. Each state has their own system for funding programs and local governments – municipal finance is never one size fits all, making the evaluation of potential changes complicated. S&P reported that, on average, US states receive more than 30% of their operating revenue from the federal government, while US local governments receive less than 5%. However, 30% of local government operating revenue comes from state sources.

States may implement budget changes to address federal funding changes, and those will trickle down to local government, as well as to educational and healthcare institutions, housing programs, and infrastructure funding (maybe upkeep of roads will be reduced), and the list goes on. In past recessions, states have made budget choices to reduce funding to some or all programs, but they have also provided support to local governments or sectors that may be more affected by economic weakness or other types of revenue shortfalls.

Pensions funding has become more disciplined because large future liabilities need to be addressed. There is more transparency in financial reporting, which allows stakeholders to keep governments accountable to citizens. A challenged revenue picture, either from federal actions or economic weakness, could make it tempting for a municipality to not fully fund annual contributions to a plan whose payments are due far into the future, in order to fund immediate needs. The stock market downturn and increasing investments in alternative assets are also elements that may affect pension funding levels and the estimation of future liabilities that need to be managed.

Tax Exemption

In addition to the challenges faced by municipalities previously noted in this commentary, the elimination of municipal bond tax exemption is being considered, which would increase financing costs for municipalities. For a detailed discussion of the issues and our view on the risk to tax exemption, see Ben Pease’s commentary “Municipal Bond Tax Exemption.”

ASCE Report Card

Every four years we await the American Society of Civil Engineers (ASCE) report card on America's infrastructure – a vast interconnected system of highways, streets, public buildings, mass transit, ports, airports, inland waterways, dams, levees, water systems, waste facilities, the electric grid, broadband networks, and other public and private facilities. As ASCE notes, maintaining these networks is essential to meet economic demands and protect public health and safety.

The average grade for infrastructure has been improving, from D+ in 2017 to C- in 2021 and to C in the 2025 report card. The improvement reflects increased investment from federal, state, and local stakeholders. However, a grade of C is considered mediocre, and continued improvement is needed. The Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act and the Inflation Reduction Act provided substantial funding; and municipal bond issuance, which funds most of our infrastructure, has averaged $450 billion a year since 2019.

Tax-exempt municipal bonds are an important tool for improving our infrastructure, and the lower financing costs made possible by federal programs make projects more affordable to taxpayers and ratepayers.

State Rating Changes

Oklahoma was upgraded to AA+ by S&P, reflecting the state’s consistently positive financial results and management's commitment to the structural stability of its finances, demonstrated by its contributions of revenue windfalls for one-time purposes and the progress made toward fully funding pension liabilities. The state has strong reserves that should help cushion cyclical revenue swings from economic or energy-related pressures.

Municipal bond upgrades continue to surpass downgrades, but the challenges faced by municipalities are numerous. The federal funding of programs and the effect of tariffs remain to be seen. We will watch developments and the reaction of state and local governments as well as healthcare and higher education institutions to the coming changes. Many munis, especially the ones that Cumberland Advisors invests in, with an average rating in the AA category, should be able to weather the storm.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Disrupted Cross-Border Freight Shipping Braces For U.S. Tariffs
data.description:
Cross-border supply chains are being tested and reshaped as US importers rush to bring goods into the US before 25% tariffs on all Canadian and Mexican goods take full effect Wednesday, along with an unknown number of potential global reciprocal tariffs.

US importers and exporters are feeling the impact of those tariffs already in the form of rising cross-border transportation costs, disrupted supply lines, and in some cases, broken relationships with suppliers and customers on the other side of the US border.

On the US-Mexico border, “there’s definitely been a sense of panic yesterday and today,” Jordan Dewart, president of Redwood Mexico, part of third-party logistics provider (3PL) Redwood Logistics, told the Journal of Commerce Tuesday. “We are seeing a really big push to get goods across the border today.”

That push has been building over the last two weeks, similar to the push or pull-forward of goods before the original March 4 deadline for the US tariffs on Canada and Mexico.

Leading up to Wednesday, only 51% of Mexican and 62% of Canadian goods have been subjected to the tariffs. That is slated to change on April 2, when 25% duties will be placed on all imports from the two largest US trading partners, except for some energy products.

Cross-border freight at ports of entry such as Laredo in Texas screeched to a halt March 4 but then gathered speed after the White House delayed tariffs on goods that complied with the US-Mexico-Canada trade agreement’s (USMCA’s) North American content standards until April 2.

“We fully expect a hold [by shippers] on crossings at the Mexican border Wednesday and potentially a longer holding pattern depending on whether the tariffs stick for a longer period,” Dewart said. Shippers can expect added delays at border crossings.

“Cross-border shipping is a highly orchestrated and complex process that requires preplanning of inbound southbound capacity to meet northbound demand,” Dewart said. “These situations really create a massive bottleneck on either side of the border.”

The 25% tariff on Mexican and Canadian goods looks increasingly like the tip of a much larger iceberg, including perhaps a universal tariff covering all US imports. President Donald Trump is expected to make an announcement on reciprocal tariffs Wednesday.

“It’s unclear whether tariffs on goods from Mexico might be reassessed, whether the current exemption for USMCA-compliant goods might expire or how soon the new tariff on auto parts will go into effect,” said Mike Burkhart, vice president of North America surface transportation at logistics provider C.H. Robinson Worldwide.

The tariff threat has tightened capacity across C.H. Robinson’s network of more than 1,000 Mexican trucking companies, “as customers hedge their bets,” by bringing more goods to the border, Burkhart said.

Dewart said many shippers are playing a “wait-and-see” game, holding inventories at the border or “in-country,” a strategy he said is not sustainable in the long term.

“Some smaller companies have even said that if tariffs go into full effect, their model will just not work, and they will have to rethink their entire business,” he said.

Overhauling supply chains

That “rethink” is already happening at many companies, according to Paul Brashier, vice president of global logistics at 3PL ITS Logistics.

“This is having a pretty large effect on North American supply chains,” Brashier told the Journal of Commerce Tuesday. “We’ve seen a lot of projects involving Mexico and Canada put on the back burner. You’re going to have complete supply chain overhauls.”

Brashier has already seen annual contracting plans change radically due to the tariffs.

“Usually at this time of the year we’re buttoning up [requests for proposals] and going into new contracts, but now we’re seeing a lot more second quarter redesign, repricing and minibids,” he said.

ITS Logistics has been grabbing all the southbound truck capacity it can over the past month to get trucks to the US-Mexico border to move northbound loads, Brashier said. But that’s difficult, as northbound loads can outnumber southbound loads by four to one.

Overall, the US freight market is still soft, despite the pull-forward of freight ahead of tariffs.

“The change in the flow is what’s creating all the work,” said Brashier. “While there’s plenty of supply, we’re changing tack and changing the way capacity moves.”

The US has seen an increase in flatbed truck demand linked to tariffs, with spot flatbed volumes and rates soaring in recent weeks.

Canadian capacity strained

On the northern US border, truck capacity is scarcer than it used to be. “Tariff uncertainty is impacting carrier supply in Canada,” Burkhart said.

“Elevated spot prices have provided short-term opportunities for carriers, but for some, that hasn’t offset the disruption of long-standing cross-border trade flows,” he said. As a result, some motor carriers are downsizing, further limiting available capacity.

Spot rates in lanes from Canada to the US have been elevated much of this year, rising as much as 75% in some cases, driven by high cross-border volumes.

As of April 1, dry-van spot rates in the busy Toronto-to-Chicago spot truck lane are 20 cents higher in a seven-day rolling average at $1.81 per mile, excluding fuel, than the March average rate of $1.61 per mile, according to DAT Freight & Analytics.

Toronto-to-Chicago dry-van spot rates are up 25% from January. Rates from Chicago to Toronto, also excluding fuel, climbed from $1.45 per mile in December to $2.01 per mile in March but are down to $1.71 on a seven-day rolling average as of April 1.

Other supply chain constraints are cutting into available truck capacity in Canada.

“For the past several weeks, rail congestion has significantly impacted intra-Canada long-haul capacity,” Burkhart said. “As a backup of container ships at many ports and rail backlogs persist, more shippers are turning to full truckload to mitigate delays.”

That’s placing additional demand on an already constrained market and creating upward pressure on truckload rates, he said.

That upward pressure could flip after tariffs are implemented and higher costs dampen consumer demand, which already appears to be falling.

“I see this as similar to a peak produce season,” Brashier said. “It’s short-lived until inventory either gets right-sized or new sourcing patterns come into play.”

Originally published in the Journal of Commerce, April 1, 2025

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Fair Isaac: Inmune To Tariffs
data.description:
The last time I wrote about Fair Isaac Corporation (NYSE:FICO) was in October 2023. Back then, it seemed the company wasn't trading at a particularly cheap valuation. In fact, I gave it a hold rating because the company was trading at 30X EBIT and had a net debt/EBITDA ratio of 2.5X. Time has passed and clearly, I was proven wrong, since the company has gone up by 98% since then, compared to a 19% increase in the S&P 500 over the same period.

Over this time, I've come to better understand the company's value creation mechanism, and it's time to update some of the numbers it has presented over the past two years. Additionally, we'll take a look at how FICO may be one of the stocks most immune to U.S. tariff policy, although it could still be affected by collateral effects.

The rest of my thesis on the company has barely changed, except that now FICO is beginning to pull the pricing lever in its Scores segment. This is a clear advantage for FICO, since, let’s remember, it went 25 years without raising the price of its credit ratings for loans. Now that it has firmly established itself as the undisputed leader in the sector and has created what is perhaps the strongest network effect there is—the one tied to language—it has free rein to raise prices with barely any impact on demand. This flows directly into the company’s margins, which we’ll later see have increased significantly.

Business Model

Let’s briefly recall what FICO is, although I strongly recommend reading my previous article to gain a deeper understanding of the company’s segments and key performance indicators (KPIs). I'll use the paragraph from my previous article, but I'll update the data and images.

The company segments its sales into two categories. The "Scores" part represents 56% of sales, and the software part represents 44%. To give you an idea of how positively the business is evolving, scores had an operating margin of 77% in 2016, and now it is 88% (I would say it is the highest I have seen in any company), and its percentage of sales has increased from 28% in 2017 to 56% now. This means that the company's total operating margins have increased from 19% to 42.7% in 7 years! This is spectacular and will also surely continue to grow since the scores segment grows faster than the software segment.

Robustness Ratio

This was also a section I included in my previous analysis.

"This concept was created by Nick Sleep. The robustness ratio is the still-untapped pricing power over the customer that a company has. That is, how much more can you raise the price of a product without affecting demand because the value you add to it is much greater"

And on this note, they mentioned the following during the conference call:

"Today, we've announced that for calendar 2025, FICO's wholesale royalty will be $4.95 per score for mortgage originations. At this new per score royalty, the amount collected by FICO will remain a small percentage, on average, about 15% of the Tri-Merge bundle cost, which typically runs $80 to well over $100." Will Lansing, CEO.

It seems we weren't far off, and FICO's untapped pricing power is still very significant.

Capital Allocation

This is one of the key points of the thesis, in order to understand FICO's value creation strategy.

The first thing to understand is that FICO has a highly recurring demand flow. The software segment is undergoing a transition to the cloud, which may reduce short-term sales but increases margins and long-term recurrence. On the other hand, it holds a 90% market share in loan ratings—a very common practice in the U.S.—which also represents highly recurring demand and a massive, untapped pricing power. More on this later.

The other point is that FICO has no reinvestment capacity within the company. Its CAPEX-to-sales ratio is 0.5%, and on top of that, it has extremely stable working capital, as we’ll see later. This makes it an asset-light company.

Thanks to this, we can understand FICO's leverage capacity. It typically maintains a Net Debt/EBITDA ratio above 2.5x, without it posing any financial issue for the company.

Since it lacks reinvestment capacity, the extremely high returns—56% ROIC and 57% ROCE, both including goodwill—are of limited use.

That’s why FICO’s way of delivering superior returns to its shareholders is by repurchasing shares using debt—without this posing a risk to the company. In fact, thanks to this approach, we can see that the number of outstanding shares has decreased by 20% over the last 7 years, which has helped both EPS and cash flow per share grow at faster rates than the nominal figures. For example, FCF has grown at a 24.7% CAGR over the past 6 years, while FCF per share has grown at 29.4%.

Remember that in the long run, stock prices always follow free cash flow per share.

The whole debt mechanism explains why the total cash used for share buybacks since 2018 has been 156% of FCF. And even with all of this, Cash and equivalents have grown at a 9% annual rate. What I don’t like about FICO is the high percentage of SBC relative to revenue and OCF. Later, I’ll show how FCF changes if we adjust for this.

The only form of reinvestment that FICO has is through its R&D. Thanks to its very dominant position in the industry, investments in this area don’t need to be massive either. Moreover, due to economies of scale, even though it invests more each year in absolute dollar terms, it represents a smaller and smaller percentage of sales. Most of these investments go to the software segment.

Financials

Let’s move on to the rest of the financial aspects. The first thing to highlight is how the growth rates accelerate as you move down the P&L, from a 9.4% 6Y CAGR in revenue to 29.4% in FCF per share.

This is due to the significant operating leverage the company has experienced over the years. For example, in 2018, the EBIT margin was 18%, and now it stands at an incredible 43%. Considering that the ratings segment is likely to continue growing faster than the software segment and enjoys much higher margins, these figures should keep trending upward.

Working capital remains stable between 5–7% of revenue and typically doesn't have significant inflows or outflows.

Let’s now take a look at the SBC aspect I mentioned earlier. FICO’s SBC represents 27% of OCF, which is a very high figure. If we adjust FCF for SBC (as shown in the chart on the left), we see the FCF margin drop from 36%, as shown in the chart on the right, to 28%.

That’s a very noticeable difference, although I believe the market isn’t factoring it in when valuing FICO. This is important—just because a financial metric is technically correct doesn’t mean the market is giving it weight or using it for valuation purposes. In my opinion, the market is focusing on FICO’s FCF per share without adjusting for SBC, which explains the company’s outstanding performance. We will need to take this into account in the valuation.

Valuation

Valuation is the most controversial aspect of FICO. I’ll use two methods to assess it. In the first one, I’ll present my base case scenario, where FICO’s free cash flow grows at 15% annually over the next 5 years, the company buys back 3% of its shares each year, and I apply a 35X multiple in 2029 (I almost never use a higher multiple than this).

If we discount these assumptions at a 12% IRR—as a minimum required return for our investment—we get a fair value for FICO of $1,158 per share. And keep in mind, we used the unadjusted FCF (not accounting for SBC), and we also didn’t factor in their net debt, which should be subtracted from the EV—but we left that out too.

Since the target price we get in a scenario that isn’t even too conservative is still more than 30% below FICO’s current market price, I’ll use a 10-year reverse DCF with a 10% IRR and a 4% terminal growth rate (TGR) to try to figure out what growth rate the market is pricing in.

Once again, I’ll use unadjusted FCF (not accounting for SBC), because as I mentioned earlier, I believe the market doesn’t typically adjust for this metric.

With all this, I estimate that the growth rate the market is pricing into FICO’s current share price ($1,700) is a 26.5% FCF CAGR over the next 10 years—which I believe is a very high rate and leaves little room for error.

On the other hand, if we don’t adjust for the company’s net debt, the growth rate implied by the market would be 25.8% FCF CAGR over the next 10 years—which, in my view, is still a very high growth rate.

Despite all this, I believe FICO is one of the best companies in the world, and just like I mentioned in my first article, even if it appears overvalued, it may still be capable of delivering positive upside surprises.

Because of that, I can’t assign the company a sell rating, and I’m giving it a Hold.

Important Graph

You already know I usually like to include this chart, and FICO’s is quite impressive.

The total return achieved by the company over the past 10 years has crushed that of any index. Moreover, it has done so with relatively mild drawdowns. Except for the drop in March 2020, the rest of the drawdowns from peak levels have never exceeded 40%. That said, the company’s current multiple is well above its historical average of 35X.

Risks

If we talk about risks, one that many companies are going to mention in their Q1 presentations starting next week will be the tariffs from the new administration.

Honestly, I don't think this will be a direct risk for FICO. More than 85% of FICO's sales come from the Americas. Moreover, since it is not a tangible product, it is not subject to tariffs—not even on the import of production goods, as it doesn't require them. It is also not heavily exposed to exchange rate fluctuations.

I’d say the main concern comes from the possibility of the U.S. entering a recession or experiencing a significant rise in inflation, which would impact American consumers and, consequently, the loans taken out for consumption. I understand the concerns, but since FICO strikes me as such a profitable company with significant untapped pricing power, I don’t think it’s a major risk and certainly not one that would threaten the company’s survival.

The other two risks that, I believe, haven't changed since my previous article are customer concentration.

"The 3 big data agencies (TransUnion, Experian and Equifax), which in turn are the owners of Vantage scores, are FICO's largest clients. The three together represented 39%, 38%, and 33% of the company's sales in the last 3 years, so they may have a high concentration of clients. 82% of sales come from the USA. These three together only represented 20% in 2017, so the concentration has doubled in six years." My previous article.

And a potential risk I see as very remote is related to regulation, although if we remember that:

"Many mortgages loans in the US include FICO ratings. According to Will Lansing in the 2022 Q4 conference call: "Finally, as you are aware, the Federal Housing Finance Agency announced that Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac have completed their validation and approval of new credit score models. I'm pleased the process has concluded and that the FICO scores have again been approved for conforming mortgages by the enterprises. This decision means that FICO Score 10T will be required to be used when available, as classic FICO is today for each conforming mortgage delivered to the enterprises."

Regulation, rather than being a risk, actually seems to be something that continues to benefit them for now.

Conclusion

I think FICO is a fantastic company, but unfortunately, that doesn't always translate into a good investment. Even though I was wrong about its valuation in my first analysis, I still don't feel comfortable paying nearly 60X for any company. If FICO were to drop to the levels mentioned in the valuation section—which would be an incredible 50% drop from its highs—I’d be very happy to add to my position. In any case, I’ll likely start covering the company here on Seeking Alpha more frequently.

data.title: The U.S. Economy's Trajectory Amid Higher Tariffs
data.description:
On 2 April, the Trump administration announced sweeping tariffs that were more aggressive than many had expected. Then on 9 April, the administration announced a 90-day pause on most of the new country-specific “reciprocal tariffs.” However, tit-for-tat retaliation has further escalated tariffs on U.S. imports from China, and vice versa.

President Donald Trump has consistently emphasized the trade deficit as a measure of unfair practices by other countries that disadvantage the U.S. Many investors, businesses, and world leaders were surprised by the administration’s apparent willingness to tolerate the ensuing near-term economic disruption and market volatility in pursuit of long-term changes to global trade dynamics. The 90-day pause creates some time for de-escalation and negotiation, but the overall direction remains clear: higher tariffs are likely here to stay.

If the tariff plan moves forward as initially announced, the near-term outcome will likely be stagflationary domestically and contractionary globally. Yet, the Federal Reserve may face challenges in aggressively cutting interest rates due to an uncomfortably large upward domestic price adjustment. These policies are likely to be deflationary for the rest of the world, resulting in fewer constraints on central banks outside the U.S. to cut rates.

Tariff announcements to date

On 2 April, Trump announced a comprehensive “reciprocal tariff” plan aimed at addressing perceived trade imbalances and unfair practices by U.S. trading partners. The plan has two main components: 1) a baseline universal 10% tariff on all imports into the U.S., and 2) additional country-specific tariffs on imports from 57 countries with significant trade deficits.

Then, on 9 April, Trump announced a 90-day pause on country-specific tariffs for those countries that did not announce retaliatory tariffs – so, most of them. But China, which responded with its own set of tariffs (34% on all goods imported from the U.S.) over the weekend, is the notable exception: As of this writing, the U.S. now imposes a 125% tariff on Chinese imports.

For Mexico and Canada, the 25% tariffs announced in early March on an estimated 50%–60% of goods imported from these countries remain in effect, and will likely be a focus of future negotiations around the U.S.–Mexico–Canada Agreement (USMCA). And many specific goods – such as steel, aluminum, passenger vehicles, lumber, pharmaceuticals, and semiconductors – are either already subject to U.S. tariffs or will be soon, according to the administration.

Overall, we estimate that if fully implemented, the tariffs announced since early February, plus the additional product tariffs that we expect will be announced soon, would together raise the effective average tariff rate on U.S. imports to levels that exceed those of the 1930s. And even if some tariffs are dialed back, it’s still very likely that the overall rate will remain dramatically higher than anything seen in decades.

Tariff outlook, near term and longer term

We believe the scope, scale, and longevity of U.S. tariffs hinge on three overarching goals of the Trump administration:

  • Reducing the U.S. trade deficit and rebalancing global trade
  • Lowering U.S. federal fiscal deficits
  • Reversing the decades-long decline in labor’s share of income

In addition to potentially raising revenue for the Treasury, the administration likely sees tariffs as a strategy to inflict enough pain on the current global trading system to compel structural changes in other economies, aiming to reduce or eliminate subsidies – whether implicit or explicit – to their manufacturing sectors and boost U.S. exports. China remains a central focus of U.S. trade policy, and in any negotiations, the administration will likely seek assurances that Chinese manufacturers can no longer use investments in “connector countries” (to use an IMF term) as a way around the U.S. direct tariffs on China.

The U.S. approach with Canada and Mexico could be informative: Imports that don't comply with the USMCA’s strict rules of origin – such as those from Chinese-owned factories built after Trump's first term – face 25% tariffs.

The administration has signaled its willingness to negotiate on trade and tariffs. Indeed, the 90-day reprieve followed a week in which many countries approached the U.S. to “negotiate a solution,” as President Trump wrote. Deals with economies where the U.S. runs a trade surplus, including the U.K. and Australia, are likely to be more easily negotiated, as well as deals with Japan and Argentina. However, tariffs targeting countries with serial trade deficits with the U.S., such as China and various countries in Europe (Germany, Ireland, Italy, Switzerland, France, Austria, and others), and Asia (Vietnam, Taiwan, South Korea, Thailand, Indonesia, and others) could be more difficult to negotiate away: These countries may need to implement structural policy and economic changes in order to reduce their serial trade deficits. Negotiations and deals are possible, but could be much more challenging for these countries.

Overall, we expect that elevated tariffs on China, a 10% baseline tariff (with the exclusions mentioned above), and various product tariffs are here to stay. Select country-specific reciprocal tariffs, even if they are implemented following the 90-day pause, could still be negotiated down.

What are the economic effects?

Assuming all of these tariffs are implemented as initially announced, we would expect the U.S. economy to experience recession and higher inflation, at least in the short run. Even if the 90-day reprieve turns into a longer stint, we still think U.S. recession odds are 50/50. Higher tariffs on U.S. imports raise costs for domestic consumers and businesses and reduce real disposable incomes and profit margins. Retaliatory measures will further damp U.S. exports. Since the tariffs are applied to producer and consumer goods alike, they will tend to make investment (as well as consumption) more expensive. In that sense, these tariffs are similar to a large and inefficiently applied consumption tax, where the only near-term winner is the U.S. government deficit.

Elevated uncertainty is likely an additional drag on growth, as businesses face little cost in delaying hiring and investment decisions. Services industries aren’t likely to be immune. Although goods industries make up only around 10% of U.S. real GDP growth (according to the Bureau of Economic Analysis), engineering a sudden stop in trade will affect services industries built around commerce: retail and wholesale trade, logistics and warehousing, trade financing, etc.

As a rule of thumb, we estimate that each 1 ppt increase in the average effective tariff rate shaves off about 0.1 ppt of growth while adding a similar amount to inflation. This calculation excludes potential offsets from federal government measures, such as remitting the additional tariff revenues back into the economy via lower income tax rates, higher subsidies, or lump sum payments.

Based on this rule of thumb, the estimated 30-ppt increase in effective U.S. tariffs (if it is implemented and maintained) likely will send the U.S. into a recession and raise near-term inflation dramatically. Given our previous baseline expectation of 2% growth and 2.5% inflation, we now expect U.S. growth to contract in the second half of the year. We estimate core CPI inflation could accelerate to 4.5%, though headline inflation may be around 1 ppt lower if the 20% decline in global energy prices (as of this writing) is sustained. While these estimates are highly variable, it’s clear the U.S. economy hasn’t seen a shock like this since the 1920s and 1930s.

A key issue in projecting the economic effects of tariff policies is understanding who is the more flexible party. Focusing on U.S. trade with China, for example, the Trump administration has made the argument that China is less flexible, and as a result will pay more of the cost of the tariff, with high tariffs benefiting the U.S. on net. The administration has indicated its view that China has few other markets to sell goods at scale, whereas the U.S. has a lot of flexibility to buy goods from domestic manufacturers and other markets.

Our view is that over the long term, assuming substantial investment in U.S. manufacturing, this might become the case. However, in the short run, we would argue the opposite is true. With many products, Chinese producers operate with monopoly-like power because they have used implicit subsidies generated from government policies and lower labor costs to largely outcompete U.S. manufacturing. Witness the decades-long reduction in U.S. manufacturing’s share of GDP coinciding with Chinese import competition. As a result, U.S. consumers today have less ability to switch to domestic supply.

Implications for the Fed

All of this puts the Fed in a tough position. Unlike 2018 and 2019, when Fed officials preemptively cut the fed funds rate in response to trade-policy-related uncertainty, we suspect they will be slower to react to economic weakness this time. The scale and scope of the tariffs (enacted and proposed) suggest a much more extensive price pass-through, which we estimate could raise inflation materially above the Fed’s 2% target (as measured by headline PCE). This outlook could constrain the Fed’s ability to cut the policy rate – barring a more material increase in the unemployment rate. Furthermore, because this is a policy-induced downturn, the Fed must also grapple with the potential for policies to be rolled back at any time – as indeed happened on 9 April.

The Fed will have to assess recession risks and inflationary risks in real time, taking into account the knock-on impacts on financial markets and consumer and business expectations. Financial conditions have tightened dramatically in the U.S. recently. However, inflation expectations surveys have also moved higher.

As a baseline scenario, we believe the Fed will likely respond in the second half of this year by cutting the policy rate as U.S. unemployment likely mounts. Ahead of that, the Fed potentially could use unconventional tools to stabilize the U.S. Treasury market, not dissimilar from the Bank of England’s bond-buying program in response to the 2022 disorderly rise in gilt yields.

Since any Fed rate cuts this year would contradict traditional Taylor rules, which could prescribe hikes amid inflation pressures, Fed communications would likely emphasize the temporary nature of the above-target inflation while highlighting that demand effects and rising unemployment tend to be more enduring.

In light of tariff revenues, should we expect larger tax cuts?

Possibly. Over the weekend, the Senate passed a budget bill, which made the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act permanent and included additional tax cuts costing $1.5 trillion over 10 years. Unlike a similar bill passed by the House earlier this year, which included $1.5 trillion in government spending cuts, the Senate version doesn’t include steep cuts to Medicaid and other programs. It’s possible that the additional tax cuts could be front-loaded, but the roughly $150 billion per year average yearly cost from the Senate bill would offset only a portion of the additional estimated $500 billion to $600 billion per year raised by tariffs. In other words, if the Senate version of the bill becomes law, then the more aggressive tax cuts could offset some of the tariff-related “tax hike,” but likely not all of it. The net effect of income tax cuts and subsidies plus tariffs amounts to a very inefficiently applied consumer or value-added tax.

Overall, we believe the U.S. fiscal impulse is likely to be negative near term, but longer-term deficits are generally still a concern. Since tariffs can be unilaterally reduced by a president anytime, the risk of this mix is skewed toward higher deficits over time.

Investment takeaways

As we discuss in our latest Cyclical Outlook, fixed income may provide a source of stability amid market volatility. Elevated uncertainty is likely to challenge the U.S. equity outperformance of recent years. Despite the recent volatility and repricing in U.S. Treasury and other fixed income markets, there is still a strong case to diversify away from highly priced U.S. equities into a broader mix of global, high quality bonds that offer attractive starting yields and a more favorable risk-adjusted profile.

Disclosures

All investments contain risk and may lose value. Investing in the bond market is subject to risks, including market, interest rate, issuer, credit, inflation risk, and liquidity risk. The value of most bonds and bond strategies are impacted by changes in interest rates. Bonds and bond strategies with longer durations tend to be more sensitive and volatile than those with shorter durations; bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise, and low interest rate environments increase this risk. Reductions in bond counterparty capacity may contribute to decreased market liquidity and increased price volatility. Bond investments may be worth more or less than the original cost when redeemed. Equities may decline in value due to both real and perceived general market, economic and industry conditions.

Statements concerning financial market trends or portfolio strategies are based on current market conditions, which will fluctuate. There is no guarantee that these investment strategies will work under all market conditions or are appropriate for all investors and each investor should evaluate their ability to invest for the long term, especially during periods of downturn in the market. Investors should consult their investment professional prior to making an investment decision. Outlook and strategies are subject to change without notice.

PIMCO as a general matter provides services to qualified institutions, financial intermediaries and institutional investors. Individual investors should contact their own financial professional to determine the most appropriate investment options for their financial situation. This material contains the opinions of the manager and such opinions are subject to change without notice. This material has been distributed for informational purposes only and should not be considered as investment advice or a recommendation of any particular security, strategy or investment product. Information contained herein has been obtained from sources believed to be reliable, but not guaranteed. No part of this material may be reproduced in any form, or referred to in any other publication, without express written permission. PIMCO is a trademark of Allianz Asset Management of America LLC in the United States and throughout the world.

CMR2025-0409-4396779

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Platinum Group Metals: I Still Don't See It
data.description:
That I can't see something, well

Given that I am not omniscient - no, really, this is true - that I cannot see something doesn't mean that it won't happen, isn't true or is a bad idea. It just means that with the data I've got in front of me and my amount of brainpower (yes, please do insert joke here) that I can't see it. So, take this for what it is, me not seeing it not a declaration that it will never happen.

Platinum Group Metals (NYSE:PLG)

I can't see how Platinum Group Metals brings their South African mine project into production. I'm not seeing that the numbers add up to make a financing decision work. As I say, this could just be me. Within the company there are no doubt finer minds than mine - certainly some better paid than me - and they seem to differ. And yet.

Now there is a get-around here, which we'll get to. But if that is used, then I tend to think that this is still something we don't want to be involved with. For even if such a workaround were used the plan would be a large bet upon future prices. Future prices that may or may not come true and, to my mind at least, have only a small chance of doing so.

The basic idea is fine

There's no doubt that the metals they claim are there. The palladium, platinum, rhodium. They're just off a long known and highly productive province. Everyone around knows how to do this mining, how to process the ore and so on. There are no technical problems here. Even the ore grade is just fine - others have mined and profited from having done so entirely similar ores.

We've not got that usual uncertainty that surrounds so many mine decisions that is.

The problem is price

PGM processing is expensive, ore grades are always low, the sheer cost of mining is therefore high. It's therefore lucky there aren't many places around the world where the mining can be done at all, this keeps prices nice and high. But the decision to go mining is driven by how high are those prices going to be?

My contention is that PLG are assuming higher prices than are going to happen. Partly because of this:

The two that actually matter - rather than being nice additions - are platinum and palladium (Pt and Pd).

So the price assumptions are higher than current prices. For Pd - and that's the bulk of the revenue - by about 15% or so and for Pt by more like 50%.

That strikes me as being notably hopeful. Note that the price assumptions are from their latest documentation release, Feb this year.

Now, these are assumptions about long-term prices - there's a 50-year mine life here in technical terms (that is, in what can be mined, not in what is economic to mine). These are also real prices, so we can expect them to rise with inflation over time, or at least, the model in use here does.

And those prices look very aggressive to me.

So why such high prices?

The assumption being made is this:

There's agreement that the catalyst market for PGMs - and that's the one big market that really matters for prices - is going to disappear. Because we're all going to be using EVs instead. True, at least if governments have their way it's going to be true.

But they're now assuming that it's not, in fact, going to be that leap to EVs. Instead, the move is going to be to hybrids. And hybrids still require a catalytic convertor, so huzzah the market for PGMs remains.

I think that's a very brave assumption. Much too brave for me.

As I've said recently:

We need to understand one thing. "Clean Air" is their business selling platinum, palladium and rhodium into the catalytic convertor business for petrol and diesel vehicles. I'm not sure whether their recycling of those is in that sector or in PGM services - that second might be refining for smaller miners. But given the revenue importance that doesn't make much difference to this next point, this worry.

That market disappears in a few years' time. Different countries have different target dates but 2030 and 2035 are bandied about. Yes, there will be the aftermarket, a vehicle sold in 2029 will likely still be on the road in 2049 and requiring the occasional replacement exhaust and cat.

But the new market? That's going to be gone. And more than that. The vast amount currently attached to vehicles of Pt, Pd and Rh is going to be coming back into the refineries and then will have - perhaps - nowhere to go. Prices are going to fall that is. It's even possible - unlikely perhaps, but possible - that the value once refined out will be lower than the cost of refining out.

That's about the refiner (of scrap and new) Johnson Matthey. But I think it stands. Or at least, it is my opinion and so it stands as my opinion here too.

The other thing we'd like to know is what is the price elasticity of the PGMS? How much do prices change given a change in demand (or supply). The best I've got is this from a decade back:

From a recent trade report we've seen recently, an extra 250,000 ounces has come onto the market. This has led to a 25 per cent fall in the price of platinum.

I agree that's very handwavey and not to be taken seriously. Other than just to say that we think that for Pt at least (and I see no reason at all why it wouldn't be true of Pd) is that demand isn't all that reactive to price. Therefore, increases in supply, reductions in demand, come through as changes in that price more than anything else.

So, the initial opinion

I'm unconvinced that the hybrids market is going to grow as this claim hopes. I think that the current stock of PGMs encapsulated in car catalysts is going to come to an end of life, be recycled and so hit the market over time. Other than that hybrids market, no one really sees a new and existing use for that amount of PGMs. So, the expectation - recall, in the absence of hybrids - is that the PGM prices will fall from current levels.

I also think that the current price estimates are a little too aggressive on the upside anyway, even for current market conditions.

So I'm unconvinced that the decision to mine will actually be taken. I lean toward the idea that it won't in fact. So, I come to the conclusion that this is one of those nice ideas, didn't work, Oh Well, things.

Except

The people behind this include:

Note that this is the project itself, not the equity of PGL.

A Japanese state-owned entity might well take a less market-oriented view of what is to happen. Could be some reasons of state to take that risk, for example.

Could be, maybe. In which case the decision to mine or not would be taken perhaps not strictly upon the economic grounds I've outlined. Perhaps, note.

But for us as investors that doesn't really help. For if an investment decision is to be taken upon not purely economic reasoning but our cash and returns are to be determined purely by economic reasoning then do we want to be involved? We, after all, don't get any return from Japanese reasons of state.

Money

The company is tiny:

And turnover:

They're awaiting that decision as to whether try to go ahead with financing for the full mine. Or not, as the case may be.

As is usual for this stage of a mining project's life - after all the research expenses, before the building expenses - they're funded by equity issues:

Platinum Group Metals (PLG) on Thursday said it has entered into a new equity distribution agreement effective as of December 5 for a new at-the-market equity program. The agreement will allow the company to distribute up to $50 million of common shares of the company under the 2024 ATM Program. The net proceeds of any such sales under the 2024 ATM Program are anticipated to be used, over the following 24 months, for the Waterberg property pre-construction site work, engineering and preparation, a potential phase one development program at the Waterberg Project, a Saudi Arabia smelter and base metal refinery definitive feasibility study, a contingency provision and general, corporate and administrative expenses.

This is just normal at this stage of the game.

We can look closer

The Q1 report:

In one sense we would say they're running on fumes. But note their biggest liability is shares issued - not really a cash issue at all. Then:

Strip out the share based - non-cash - stuff, and they're running on about 6 months' money. Which isn't a lot, obviously.

In cash terms they require some $1 million (that's a good enough level of accuracy) a quarter. Which, at current market capitalisation is about 1%. 1% per quarter dilution just to keep going? Well, if we're honest about it, that's not bad for a company at this stage. As I've said before, they're paying themselves a lot less than some exploration miner management does.

From the Q1:

On November 13, 2024, the Company filed a final short form base shelf prospectus with the securities regulatory authorities in each of the provinces and territories of Canada and a corresponding registration statement on Form F-10 with the United States Securities and Exchange Commission. On December 5, 2024 the Company entered into an Equity Distribution Agreement for a new at-the-market equity program. Management believes the Company will be required to source additional financing by way of private or public offerings of equity to meet the Company’s obligations and continue operations for at least the next twelve months. These conditions indicate the existence of material uncertainties that raise substantial doubt upon the Company’s ability to continue as a going concern.

Of course dilution is bad. But, as I say, this level of dilution - assuming they only issue at the market what is absolutely necessary - is pretty low.

This is the other issue in that Q1:

The continued operations of the Company and the recoverability of the amounts shown for mineral properties is dependent upon the ability of the Company to obtain the necessary financing to complete the development of the Waterberg Project and bring it to future profitable production.

Everything else, all other projects, are an irrelevance.

So, it all comes down to, well, is that project going to gain an investment decision? On the bare numbers, I think that unlikely - and that's to be kind. On the other hand, given the shareholder base, who is going to take a longer-term view and also, perhaps, be subject to the sunk cost fallacy? That is, I think, the calculation here.

As a pure project being promoted by an entirely independent then no - to my mind, to my mind. Given governmental involvement. Well?

Why am I wrong?

There could be a new use for PGMs. There could be that growth in the hybrid market to maintain current usage patterns. Both are possible, however unlikely I might think them.

Either of those would increase PGM prices and so make this project look much better. Any other reason for PGM prices to improve - say, really stinging trade sanctions on Norilsk, which produces much palladium, would work for as long as said sanctions did too.

My opinion

I'm very much a bear on future platinum group metals demand. I doubt that the hybrid car market is going to be as large as is presumed here - some jurisdictions are insistent that EV means EV only, not hybrid, for example. We all did think that perhaps fuel cells - PEMs, which use PGMs - might become a player and despite my personal liking for that technology, I think that the push behind EVs has meant that won't happen.

So, I think there's that bolus of PGMs coming out of the recycling of catalytic convertors, without these being a replacement use for that stock. I thus think that PGM prices are going to go down, not up as is assumed here.

It's possible that the project still goes ahead on strategic grounds, but I don't think that aids us as investors.

So, despite the passage of time and greater clarity, I'm still out.

data.title: Camtek: Strong Focus In HPC Market
data.description:
By Anthony Goh, Senior Investment Research Analyst @ Khaveen Investments & Min Shean Chong, Investment Research Intern @ Khaveen Investments

Camtek Ltd. (CAMT) is a developer and manufacturer of metrology and inspection equipment for semicon companies. Its customers include IDMs, OSATs, and foundries. Its growth has been nothing short of impressive, with a 10-year average of 19.4%, which prompted us to examine the company further to determine whether it has competitive advantages driving its performance. We first analyze Camtek's market share trend in the overall semicon equipment market and identify its competitors. We then analyze its business segments and identify if any particular segment is driving strong growth and assess whether the company has a competitive advantage in that segment.

Semicon Equipment Market Share

We first examine the company’s market share in the semicon equipment market to assess its competitive positioning against top semicon equipment players.

*2024 data is prorated

From the chart, Camtek is a small player with only a tiny market share of 0.4%, which pales in comparison with the larger players like Applied Materials (AMAT), Tokyo Electron (OTCPK:TOELF), Lam Research (LRCX), ASML (ASML), and KLA (KLAC). But Camtek’s market share doubled between 2016 and 2024 from only 0.2%, highlighting its growth performance.

We identified all the submarkets within the semicon equipment market and compiled their respective market shares. We then categorized the major players in the semicon equipment market according to the identified submarkets. According to the table, deposition, patterning, and etch technologies account for the top three largest shares in the semicon equipment market. ASML (18.5% share), one of the leading players in the market, specializes in lithography patterning. Lam Research (8.1% share) and Tokyo Electron (12.8% share) are specialized in deposition and etch technology. Applied Materials (17.4% share), having the broadest portfolio in the market, is also engaged in all three of the key technologies mentioned. Camtek and Onto Innovation, which each hold market shares of 0.4% and 0.8%, primarily focus on the niche technology of metrology and inspection. While KLA (6.9% share) is one of the key players in metrology & inspection, it also engages in deposition and etch technology, which we believe explains its much higher market share compared to Camtek and Onto (ONTO). So we believe Camtek’s small market share is due to its focus on a relatively smaller submarket (process control and metrology). Unlike its competitors, Camtek does not engage in other submarkets within the equipment manufacturing market. With larger competitors also competing in the same submarket as Camtek, we believe its overall semicon equipment market share growth opportunity is limited.

We overall believe that although Camtek has a relatively low market share in the semicon equipment market, this is primarily due to its focus on metrology and inspection technology. The key players in this segment include Onto, KLA, and Applied Materials. According to Camtek’s investor presentation, the company claimed to have over 250 customers, including “all the industry’s leading foundries, IDM and OSATs”. With a wide customer base, we believe Camtek is well-positioned to continue increasing its market share, especially given the rising demand for metrology and inspection solutions due to advancements in semicon technology.

HPC Market Driving Growth

We next examine the business segments of the company closely to determine which particular segment is driving its strong revenue performance.

Based on the chart, the company’s Advanced Interconnect Packaging Applications segment has consistently increased its share of overall revenue, accounting for 70% of total revenue in 2024. This segment has experienced high growth, averaging 29% since 2021. The Compound Semiconductor, CIS, and Process Control Application segment grew by 2% on average in the same period. We therefore believe the Advanced Interconnect Packaging Applications segment is driving the growth of the company as it accounts for the largest share of total revenue and revenue from this segment has been growing at a fast rate.

The HPC architecture primarily consists of chiplets and HBM. Heterogenous integration technology is used to integrate HBM on the chiplet, with copper microbumps providing the “required vertical metal device-to-device interconnections into a single, integrated product”, according to 3D InCites.

According to Semiconductor Engineering, in order to increase "power and signal connection density required by memory, high-performance computing, and mobile computing devices”, copper microbumps sizes have been increasingly scaled down. But 3D InCites claimed that “microbumps begin to run into yield and reliability challenges” once the bonding pitch is below 10µm. Semiconductor Engineering stated that “smaller pitches require more stringent control of bump diameter and height control”. Camtek’s products, such as the Eagle-AP, could inspect bumps down to 2µm. The 3D metrology capabilities of this system could provide bump height measurements, while its 2D metrology capabilities could analyze the diameter, width, and length of the bumps. We believe this indicates that the company’s products are able to address the challenges of inspecting and measuring bump dimensions. We thus believe this transition is increasing the demand for metrology and inspection processes of the company, especially in the HPC market, thereby driving revenue growth in the Advanced Interconnect Packaging Applications segment.

Within the Advanced Interconnect Packaging Applications segment with HPC representing the largest share of the segment, we believe Camtek could capitalize on higher demand for metrology and inspection processes with its new Hawk and Eagle G5 products. In its latest earnings briefing, management highlighted “Eagle G5 is currently in operation at multiple customer’s production lines,” and that “Hawk has successfully completed qualifications with several customers”, indicating positive progress. According to Precedence Research, the advanced packaging market is projected with a CAGR of 8.5% and the HPC market at a CAGR of 9.3%. Despite this anticipated growth in both markets, the average revenue growth of the company’s Advanced Interconnect Packaging Applications segment stands at a much higher rate of 31%. This strong growth rate suggests that the company’s outperformance is not only attributed to market growth but may also be driven by its unique competitive advantages.

Camtek’s Competitive Advantage

As we have identified that the company’s growth is not driven by market growth, we compared the company’s product offerings with its competitors to determine if it has a competitive advantage in the metrology & inspection market.

We compared the products offered by competitors across the value chain of advanced packaging, which includes defect inspection, automated defect inspection, lithography, high-volume manufacturing metrology, and metal film metrology. Camtek ranks second in product breadth among competitors, following Onto. While Camtek does not offer as many product categories as Onto, which encompasses all these areas, Camtek lacks only Lithography solutions.

Comparing the number of technologies featured in the metrology and inspection solutions offered by competitors, Onto has the highest number of technologies, followed by Camtek, Applied Materials, and KLA. Based on this, we rank Camtek as 2nd trailing only Onto. As highlighted above, Camtek has launched new Eagle and Hawk products targeting HPC, which could further solidify the company’s product portfolio. Onto on the other hand, has only one product (Firefly G3) for the HPC market, which shows Camtek’s strength for HPC.

We thus believe Camtek is competitively positioned in the market, ranking second only to Onto in terms of product breadth and number of featured technologies in its inspection and metrology solutions. We believe Camtek's primary competitive advantage lies in its strong focus on the HPC market, which has become a growth driver for the segment, contributing approximately 50% to the company’s total revenue. Meanwhile Onto’s focus is on the power device market, which has grown by 40% in revenue in 2023. We believe Camtek’s continuous effort to develop products for the HPC market positions it to capture the increasing demand for inspection and metrology solutions in this market, as evidenced by the initial order demand for its upcoming product line.

Risk: Trade Policies Uncertainties

The announced trade tariffs have led to heightened uncertainty in the markets, Camtek is headquartered and manufactures in Israel, but has limited exposure to the US which accounted for only 6.8% of its revenues in 2024. That said, we believe the uncertain business environment could lead to delays in purchases by the company’s customers, which could impact Camtek in the near term.

Verdict

While Camtek is a small player in the overall semicon equipment market, it specializes in the niche metrology and inspection business market which is smaller compared to deposition, etching, and lithography. Despite Camtek’s low market share in the semicon equipment market, we believe its wide customer base along with relatively few competitors would allow it to compete effectively with its focused specialization in metrology and inspection technology, given that more demand for this solution is needed with the advancement in semicon technologies. We view its exposure to the HPC market via its Advanced Interconnect Packaging Applications segment, in particular, to allow it to capitalize on the trend towards smaller bump sizes for increased power and signal density, as well as its new product launches with Hawk and Eagle G5 could enable the company to continue benefiting from this growth opportunity.

We forecast the revenue for each segment of the company in 2024 based on the respective historical growth rates in the past 3 years, deriving a total forward average of 27% through 2027 and 25% in 2025, driven by increasing demand for metrology and inspection solutions in the HPC market, given the industry is transitioning to smaller bump sizes and the company has been developing new products to address challenges faced by customers during this transition. Our growth forecasts for 2025 are in line with management guidance for Q1 2025 of a 25% YoY growth based on its pipeline. Given the uncertainty with trade policies, we will monitor management’s guidance for the rest of the year in the next earnings call and identify signs of whether it could maintain its growth momentum supported by its order pipeline.

Based on our DCF model using a discount rate of 12% (company’s WACC), we derived a price target of $105.24 based on the US-only semicon equipment manufacturers’ 5-year EV/EBITDA of 20.49x. This translates to a massive upside of 90.75% given the decline of its share price by 32% YTD due to the market selloff. With our positive outlook supported by HPC growth, we rate the company as a Strong Buy.

data.title: Canon: Spotlight On Geographical Exposure And Long-Term Goal
data.description:
I continue to have a neutral view of Canon (OTCPK:CAJFF) (OTCPK:CAJPY) (7751.JP).

My previous September 12, 2024 article highlighted CAJPY's business improvement measures and cash distribution policy.

The company's positive prospects for the long run and its revenue concentration risk are detailed in this write-up.

U.S. Tariffs Might Hurt CAJFF's Near-Term Results

CAJPY derived almost one-third (source: S&P Capital IQ) of its topline from the "Americas" segment last year. This was also its second best-performing region ("after Asia & Oceania") in FY24 with a 9% sales increase.
